feed.title: emailaddress.horse
feed.subtitle: John Bannister's personal website, with thoughts on indie RPGs and web development.

entry.title: A game is brewing
entry.content:
There’s a game brewing in the back of my head. A game where you protect your village, your community. Adventures come from threats and opportunities for your settlement, to protect and support their resources. The intention is to prosper and thrive, but not needlessly grow and excessively consume.

The idea is dancing between a few games I admire. The simplicity of Mausritter that gives enough mechanical crunch but stands back for characterful roleplay. The community focus and tone of Stonetop that finds modest ambitions and a focus on how you help those who don’t adventure. The character creation options of Down We Go which does away with complex classes whilst still giving some sense of archetypes and play styles.

Loosely, I think character abilities and options are governed by your settlement. The settlement has resources it uses to survive, and over time those resources are put at risk. Perhaps a natural event disrupts them, or maybe more malicious interruptions from unfriendly factions. Adventures are emergent from the needs of the settlement and the world is a dangerous place.
entry.summary: There’s a game brewing in the back of my head. A game where you protect your village, your community. Adventures come from threats and opportunities for your settlement, to protect and support their resources. The intention is to prosper and thrive, but not needlessly grow and excessively consume.

entry.title: CONFIDENCE/ANXIETY
entry.content:
Over the weekend, I finished off an idea I had been playing with for the Tiny Library Jam. In the last few weeks, my therapist explained what anxiety was. It had been floating around for so long that I didn’t realise it was a nameable thing.

The exciting realisation was that anxiety was all about my perception of my actions, not whether or not they were valuable contributions.

So I present CONFIDENCE/ANXIETY, a simple hack for any RPG that uses a dice-roll for a skill-check or save:

CONFIDENCE/ANXIETY on Itch.io
entry.summary: Over the weekend, I finished off an idea I had been playing with for the Tiny Library Jam. In the last few weeks, my therapist explained what anxiety was. It had been floating around for so long that I didn’t realise it was a nameable thing.

entry.title: I can’t stop thinking about Mausritter

link.title: I can’t stop thinking about Mausritter

entry.content:
It’s a huge and dangerous world out there, and it does not look kindly on a small mouse. But if you are very brave and very clever and just a bit lucky, you might be able to survive. And if you survive long enough, you might even become a hero amongst mice.

Mausritter a simple RPG about mice having adventures and it lives in my head now. This post is an attempt to understand how it got there and why I’m not letting it leave.

Everything is about what is happening now.

You don’t make skill checks which succeed or fail - you make saves. So action always moves forwards, you just have to hope your mouse survives.

You don’t have a complicated character. You get a few interesting prompts: a birthsign with an associated disposition; some phsyical characteristics; and a background which gives you two items. Very limited choices. The interesting thing about a character is what they do in this game, not what they did.

You keep track of time. Skipping an opportunity to rest, or wasting time in an adventure site, has ramifications. Nothing hugely punishing, but the decision you make affect you later. So your decisions count.

Mausritter is not unique in this regard, a lot of other OSR games use similar mechanics. But this game has such a focused way of delivering this sense of presence. The design, layout, writing and art all bring it back to this moment. Even the random tables give you things to respond to immediately, rather than referring back to lore (you skimmed) in other parts of the book.

I don’t think this captures everything I love about this game. But I do love it.
entry.summary: It’s a huge and dangerous world out there, and it does not look kindly on a small mouse. But if you are very brave and very clever and just a bit lucky, you might be able to survive. And if you survive long enough, you might even become a hero amongst mice.

entry.title: Public accountability
entry.content:
I’m trying to building some of my routine and habits back up. The pandemic has shaken all of them up. But as I research more about a possible ADHD diagnosis, it seems important to do this personal gardening work to stop everything getting mentally overgrown.

It’s a tortured metaphor. But I’m trying to get some structure back. I’m starting with meditation. Specifically mindfulness as peddled by Headspace. Without a decent routine to hook this into, I’m turning to other powerful tools. And the biggest hammer in that box is accountability.

So I’ve setup a page on this site where I will be sharing my habit tracking. It will help for two reasons:

  • It was fun to make the page and I look forward to seeing how it grows and how I can improve it;
  • It is embarrassing to encourage people to take a look and then see that I have failed.

So please help me stick with my habits! Encouragement and/or nudges will be greatly appreciated.
entry.summary: I’m trying to building some of my routine and habits back up. The pandemic has shaken all of them up. But as I research more about a possible ADHD diagnosis, it seems important to do this personal gardening work to stop everything getting mentally overgrown.

entry.title: May 2021 Reading
entry.content:
I shared my March and April’s reading on Twitter. Now I’m gonna share it here.

Books

How Do You Live? by Genzaburo Yoshino — A pleasant story about a young boy in Japan in the 30s. The reading was gentle and held a low level of interest, but it became much more meaningful as I learned more about the context Yoshino was writing in from Bruno Navasky’s translator’s notes.

No One Is Talking About This by Patricia Lockwood — Of course it reads like tweets to start with, Lockwood is a master of that form. Here she collides that overwhelming digital world against a real life that cannot be distracted from. I loved it.

The Employees by Olga Ravn — What’s it like working on a space ship with androids? And realising everything about your existence is in support of your employer? A wealth of interesting ideas in here. Perfect inspiration for sci-fi workplace adventuring.

Games

The Vast In The Dark by Feral Indie Studio — A really evocative setting full of brutalist grey. Probably an excellent excursion, but the overwhelming sense of despair means you probably want an exit from it into another world. And a personal frustration that the origins of brutalist architecture are more around building community but they’re still used as a shorthand for alienation.

Mausritter by Losing Games — Come be a mouse with me and we’ll explore villages in tree stumps and fight off marauding cats. I love this game. It achieves a really simple gameplay experience and is one of those worlds which is relatable enough to freely play in without needing any further context. Just pretend to be tiny in your garden!

Aether Operations by Floating Chair Club — One folded piece of paper taking you through the oddest adventure game. Reclaiming mundane relics in the realms of meat, bone, brain and spirit. I don’t know how to play this and I don’t know what it would look like if anyone did. But you really can do anything with games.

Brutal Quest by Mammoth Miniatures — Took a wild foray into wargaming and was a little disappointed. I have nothing to compare this to so can’t really rate this as a wargaming ruleset itself, but it just seems to have all the measuring and calculating I got bored of with D&D.

Cities of the Dying Land by Reverend Uncle Bastard — A pamphlet of random prompts and inspiration. When I make my own clothbound custom GM screen, there will be a little pocket specifically sized to fit this.

The month was light on books but heavier on games. I felt the need to get through my pile! But there is still a long list to go!
entry.summary: I shared my March and April’s reading on Twitter. Now I’m gonna share it here.
